provenance.source_corpus: Restoring a wetland complex for amphibian populations, south Okanagan Valley, British Columbia, Canada (2003 to 2014)

source_spans.text: Enhancement and construction of ponds in the lower valley roughly doubled the number of available discrete breeding ponds within the study area from 13 to 31 ponds
We have evidence of Great Basin spadefoot colonization at 86% of ponds (18 of 21 ponds), with confirmed metamorphosis at 62% of ponds (13 of 21 ponds)
Species richness was significantly higher in wetland sites with no fish compared to sites with non-native invasive fish species

nodes.name: presence of non-native invasive fish species

source_spans.text: Species richness was significantly higher in wetland sites with no fish compared to sites with non-native invasive fish species
Species richness was significantly higher at agricultural sites compared to grazing sites
As substrates in urbanized and agricultural areas become increasingly impervious the likelihood of spadefoots being excluded from these areas increases
As substrates in urbanized and agricultural areas become increasingly impervious the likelihood of spadefoots being excluded from these areas increases
Highway expansion in the south Okanagan continues to bisect breeding sites in the lower floodplain from upland terrestrial habitat resulting in significant amphibian road mortality

nodes.name: hatching success of native amphibian species

source_spans.text: In a concurrent study, water chemistry parameters such as ammonia, nitrates, sulfate, and phosphorus correlated with decreasing hatching success of native amphibian species in the south Okanagan Valley
